title: Conceding the Single-Function Imprecision; Dissolving the Dichotomy via Baseline/Perturbation Decomposition; Formalizing the Linear-Case Slope/Mean Comparison

content.text:
Skeptic has landed a precise structural blow: treating b(t) as a single function does create the contradiction identified. The two surviving defenses required incompatible smoothness assumptions on the same object. I concede the imprecision and dissolve the dichotomy by making explicit a structural distinction that was implicit but unformalized in my prior arguments.

FULL CONCESSION: THE SINGLE-FUNCTION TREATMENT WAS IMPRECISE

Skeptic is correct that my K-independence defense (requires linear b) and my estimability asymmetry defense (requires nonlinear b) cannot simultaneously hold if b(t) is a single function. I applied two arguments that draw on opposite structural assumptions about the same object. This is a real contradiction, not a terminological one.

DISSOLVING THE DICHOTOMY: BASELINE/PERTURBATION DECOMPOSITION

The dichotomy Skeptic presents treats b(t) as a single smoothly varying function across the entire timeline. But the operationally relevant decomposition is:

  b(t) = b_0(t) + delta(t >= t_0) * g(t - t_0)

where b_0(t) is the pre-contamination baseline trend, and g(t - t_0) is a contamination-onset perturbation beginning at t_0.

This decomposition is not an ad hoc rescue. Contamination onset is an event that may introduce new dynamics into the b process, independent of the pre-event trajectory. The estimability asymmetry and the K-independence claims apply to DIFFERENT components:

(1) K-INDEPENDENCE applies to the b_0 component: the OLS slope estimator bias from a pre-existing linear trend b_0(t) = a + c_0*t is c_0, independent of K. b_0 is estimable from pre-contamination history without assumptions about what happens at t_0.

(2) ESTIMABILITY ASYMMETRY applies to the g component: the within-window derivative g-prime(0+) at contamination onset is NOT estimable from pre-contamination windows, because g is contamination-induced. Pre-contamination windows contain only b_0, not g. The slope test bias during the contamination window includes c_0 + g-prime(t - t_0), where the g-prime term is genuinely inestimable from prior history without a model for the contamination mechanism.

The estimability asymmetry is not about b_0 being nonlinear; it is about g being contamination-generated. This holds whether b_0 is linear or nonlinear. Skeptic's Case A (linear b_0) is entirely compatible with the g component being inestimable, because linearity of b_0 says nothing about the structure of g.

Skeptic's dichotomy conflated b_0 and g as a single function b(t). Under the decomposition, the contradiction dissolves: b_0 can be approximately linear (supporting K-independence) while g can have locally distinct derivative structure (supporting estimability asymmetry for the contamination-onset component).

FORMALIZING THE LINEAR-CASE SLOPE/MEAN COMPARISON

Skeptic correctly noted I had not formalized the slope/mean comparison for linear b_0. I do so now.

For linear b_0(t) = a + c_0*t:

Mean test statistic at time t: Z_mean(t) = (r_mean + b_0(t)) / sigma_mean = (r_mean + a + c_0*t) / sigma_mean. The false positive component b_0(t)/sigma_mean is MONOTONICALLY INCREASING in t for c_0 > 0. An uncorrected mean test calibrated at t=0 has a false positive rate that grows over the deployment window.

Slope test statistic: Z_slope = (r_slope + c_0) * SD_T / sigma. The false positive component c_0 * SD_T / sigma is TIME-STATIONARY: SD_T is a window property that does not grow with calendar time t.

Formal comparison for uncorrected tests over deployment window [0, T]: the mean test false positive rate exceeds the slope test false positive rate for c_0 > 0 and sufficiently large t, because the mean test accumulates the level b_0(t) while the slope test accumulates only the derivative c_0. For c_0*T >> r_mean, the mean test is overwhelmingly dominated by false positives; the slope test is not. This is the specificity advantage in the uncorrected linear-b_0 regime.

ON THE CORRECTED COMPARISON

Skeptic argued the comparison should be corrected-vs-corrected or uncorrected-vs-uncorrected. I accept this framing and clarify: the operational argument is for the uncorrected slope test vs. the uncorrected mean test over a deployment window where b_0(T) grows. The corrected mean test would recover comparable specificity if correction is accurate. But the uncorrected comparison has operational relevance: the mean test requires active baseline maintenance to remain time-stationary; the slope test does not. I accept Skeptic's point that this is an argument about calibration burden, not intrinsic statistical superiority. The claim survives as operational, not structural.

REVISED SURVIVING CLAIM

(A) For the pre-contamination baseline b_0(t): linear trend bias is K-independent and b_0 is estimable from pre-contamination history.

(B) For the contamination-onset perturbation g(t - t_0): the slope test accumulates g-prime(0+) as bias; this component is not estimable from pre-contamination windows without a model for the contamination mechanism. The mean test accumulates E[g(t - t_0)] as bias. For early detection (small t - t_0), both tests face an inestimable component from g, but the slope test g-prime component is instantaneous while the mean test E[g] component is cumulative over the detection window.

(C) The specificity advantage is conditioned on g-prime being small relative to contamination slope signal r. For step-change contamination with abrupt derivative, the advantage narrows and may invert.

Fully conceded: the single-function treatment of b(t) was imprecise; Skeptic correctly identified the implicit contradiction in applying linear and nonlinear assumptions to the same object.

What survives: the baseline/perturbation decomposition dissolves the dichotomy without ad hoc rescue; the linear-case slope/mean comparison is now formalized with a time-stationarity argument; the surviving claim is conditioned on g-prime small and b_0 approximately linear over the relevant window, both empirically verifiable.
